transcript.text: Thank joining me.
How are you doing, Tyler?
Hey, no worries, Bobby.
I'm doing well.
How was your weekend?
It was good.
Where are you based?
I was going give a weather review, but you might also be based in the Bay Area.
Yeah, I'm in Houston.
I lived in SF for three years, and it was like in the 80s this weekend.
I don't know what's going on.
Okay, nice.
That's very different than here.
Ankur and I were, sorry, we were running late from a different call, but it was raining most of the weekend, so very different.
But yeah, great to meet you.
Great to meet you, yeah.
Tyler.
Yeah.
Would it be helpful if we did our backgrounds and intros, give you an overview on Liberty Global, and then we'd love to hear more about you and what your buildings.
sound good?
Yeah, that sounds great, and I'll ping Marcel to see if he can make it, but if not, no worries.
I'm the founding AE here, and I'm pretty much always taking these first and second calls to free up time for Marcel's calendar.
So I
I think he is supposed to join, but if not, you know, you can work with me today and have the overview conversation.
Great.
Yeah, sounds great.
Yeah, wonderful.
Ankur, do you want to go first?
You can go.
Why don't you take off?
Okay, perfect.
Yeah, great.
So, Bobby Montiel, I'm a principal here on the Tech Ventures team in Palo Alto.
So, I have been on this team for about two years, investing in Venture for about five.
I was formerly at March Capital, which invested in similar stages and themes as Liberty Global, down in L.A., institutional firm.

And before that, I worked in investment banking for a few years, doing tech specifically, tech enterprise software, and then also worked at Google for five years as a PMM for Google Plan and Google Maps, so have some go-to-market experience there.
And then, Ankur, why don't you go next?
Yeah.
I'm happy you can give an overview on Liberty.
Yeah, and Tyler, nice to meet you.
Ankur Prakash, I'm one of the partners in the team here.
I started with Liberty.
Liberty about 13 years back, a little over 13 years back, and my background is largely investing in enterprise software.
So for all the way from like data infrastructure to cybersecurity to, you know, even cloud and cloud compliance, I've done sort of most of that investing here.
Prior to Liberty, I was an operator.
I've worked mostly in the, I would say, the networking infrastructure space, a company like Cisco and Juniper and Alcatel and a few startups, and that's not even the early 2000s, yeah.
Fantastic.
Yeah, great to meet you both.
And Marcel, he did respond to, I texted him, he responded, said, tell him I have to run, but we can reschedule.
But at the same time, you I can give you some initial context and at least answer some of your questions that came to mind.
Quick, quick intro on me.
Before GrowthX, I started my career in strategy consulting with PwC.
And was at Segment as an Enterprise Account Executive into the acquisition by Twilio.
And then I was the founding AE at a company called Air.
They're in like the creative operations space and was an Enterprise AE at a different content marketing software company called Seros.
So basically I've been selling to marketers and creatives and had the consulting background.
So Marcel, you know, liked me as the founding AE here where we have this blend of like services and software.
I don't have a ton of context, but I know there was an intro from Madrona for this call.
I guess, how can I be most helpful, you know, until we can get Marcel into the conversation with you both?
Bobby, you want to do a quick intro on Liberty and how we, yeah.
Yeah, yeah, that sounds good.
So we're on the Tech Mentors team and our parent company, Liberty Global, it's really like a holding company.
So you can think of it as like a Berkshire Hathaway.
It's multinational and our roots are in mobile and telecom.
So I like to describe it.
Sort of as a multinational TMT holding company with true assets across, you know, telecom, media, and other technology.
And so, for example, we own and operate Europe's largest telcos, things like Virgin Media, O2, Telenet.
And then we also have media entities like Formula E and then some infrastructure assets.
And we also, through our sister company, Liberty Media, have close working relationships with some of the companies under that holding company.
Both were started by John Malone, who was our chairperson until basically like last week.
And so they have really close DNA.
And how we work with our investments.
And so we've made over 75 investments across our portfolio, have over $3 billion AUM on our venture side.
And then when we can, we like to become a customer, help make introductions across our family of brands to help, you know, grow alongside our portfolio companies.
Yeah.

And Tyler, when Karan made the introduction, he kind of knew a little bit about sort of the brands that are in the broader Liberty footprint, Virgin, O2, Formula E, Formula One, TripAdvisor, things like that.
He felt that maybe there's an opportunity for us to help you guys get into some of those brands.
But even though, you know, our mandate is really to take an investment position in companies off your stage.
And so the idea was really to explore both the investment conversation and the commercial conversation eventually, right?
That's how the introduction started.
So, yeah, mean, given where you're, you know, would love to know a little bit about GrowthX.
You know, I've heard from Karan, but he's an investor and a board member.
You know, obviously, I would love to hear from you guys, like, as in how you guys are approaching the market, what market are you looking at?
And what?
What's your ideal customers and things like that, yeah.
Yeah, 100%.

Hopefully I can be helpful in lieu of like the conversation with Marcel, but the high level on GrowthX, we're really working with our clients as an end-to-end partner and helping them execute on both SEO and GEO strategy, getting to a place where we're actually staging and publishing content for them daily, and then providing all of the LLM monitoring and visibility on the content that we're creating.
So, you you may have heard of like Profound, right, as like one of those LLM monitoring platforms.
What partners get out of GrowthX is we're like their SEO agency, but we're also this extension of their content team.
And we've built these very sophisticated AI workflows in our platform, which we're actually opening up to clients soon.
And our team is, you know, experts plus AI workflows.
Creating this content.
And so all the people on our content team, for example, you know, we've got our chief content officer who was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch for over a decade.
And then we've got folks who have basically run SEO and content marketing for different organizations like TripAdvisor, Fanatics, SEMrush, Ahrefs.
And so that's really our approach is, you know, Marcel in his career, he's always believed brands should be publishers.
He's never spent much on paid.
Every stop in his journey, you he's got a different story about, like, the content engine that he's built.

And so the idea is that, you know, we can basically scale quality by doing a lot of context engineering and creating these artifacts around the company, the ICP or personas that they go after, their brand voice and guidelines.
And we set up our engagements where there's this eight-week strategy sprint and then a 12-month growth execution.
And so, you know, we just really try to be, like, audience first in our approach.

And have found that, you know, when we create content in this way, when we're able to use these workflows, where we can make modifications really quickly from client to client, and we can create content in a way that is like more consumable and more built for LLMs, it just differentiates, you know, our approach against a company buying like a workflow orchestration tool or partnering with a traditional agency.
So the vision is, you know, definitely that we are services as software, essentially, and building a platform in parallel.
Can you talk a little bit about the broad market of SEO versus GEO now?
I mean, do you guys see SEO as eventually a sustainable model going forward?
Or, mean, search is changing dramatically now.
Yeah, maybe who you would like to compete Yeah.
Compete against.
Yeah.
really
Yeah, for sure.

You know, I think, and Marcel would be the best person to answer this question, but the perspective is, right, like, traditional search isn't going away, but it's rapidly being, like, replaced by ChatGPT, by AI mode in Google search.
You know, they say that, like, by 2027 and that date, it keeps moving, like, earlier and earlier.
think it was, like, 2028 to start, right?
Now it's, like, 2027.
It'll probably be, like, end of 2026 that AI search passes, AI search visits pass traditional search visits.
But, you know, the approach isn't necessarily that different, right?
On the SEO side, you're thinking about keywords and you're creating content that's aligned to the search volume for those keywords and is looking at competitor content.
But when you think about AI search, and this is, you know, a lot of, like, our philosophy, what are all the questions that...
Your ICP might have, right?
What are all the things that they might care about?
And I can show you a good example that touches on this a little bit more deeply.

So, you know, a lot of the content that we do is editorial in nature because you need to have a larger surface area of content for AI search when anyone can go in, right, and basically provide their life story and look for answers about the best software.
You've also got, right, the first sales call happening in AI search.
There's, you know, prospects.
We're seeing it as, like, expert advice, and they show up to your website.
They're 4.4 times more likely to convert.
They spend three times as long.
And so, you know, Augment Code is in the enterprise AI coding space, and a lot of what we've done is help solidify them as the expert when it comes to AI coding.
And so, you know, there are these tool comparisons that are connected to their competitors.
There's thought leadership around how should I leverage AI coding at the enterprise level?
And what we're doing here is end-to-end, we're creating the images, we're cross-linking, which definitely is helpful for LLMs in indexing your content.
We've got code snippets in some of this content.
And so we'll start with editorial as our foundation.
But then from there, we have the ability to think about programmatic, different work streams.
We've built, like, newsletters for clients.
We've built tools and calculators for clients.
And it really just comes down to setting up these pipelines, right, in our platform so that we can scale to different use cases.
And so with, you know, Webflow, we've built a lot of their integrations pages.
We've built definitions around REST APIs, composable commerce.
And then if you look at some of our other examples with Lovable, we're building out this templates library for them.
And we're interacting with their agents.

API to automatically generate these presentation templates, optimize them for SEO and GEO with all of the different use cases, right, all of the different questions that can be answered by an example.
And then for Ramp, we've got this programmatic use case where, you know, Ramp wants to be the authority for anything business spending.
And so every page in Ramp's vendor directory here, we've created and we've populated it with their internal spend data.
And so, you know, this is a way for Ramp to start showing up in searches that you wouldn't necessarily think about top of mind, but their audiences definitely think about it, thinking about it.
What are the alternatives to the most popular tools?
You know, why should I choose Airtable versus ClickUp, for example?
And so the idea is, as you guys are creating and populating a lot of this content, then the LLMs are going to come and train or learn about this eventually.
a news.
might but
And that's how it starts showing up in search.
Who else?
It's more so just thinking about, right, what are the questions that someone might have that can be connected to the value propositions that your products or services solve for?
And how can we systematically create the best answers to those questions?
No, go ahead, Bobby.
I was just asking, you're also building the creative, so the content is, you are owning that, or how does that work?
Yes, we'll do the image generation for the cover images of all the content, so, you know, everything that you see on this initial guides page.
We'll do infographics within the content as well.
And then we'll also do all the last mile execution, so the staging and publishing, we handle that for our customers and can automate that part of the.
And so the end-to-end nature, I think, is a big differentiator for us against, you know, other agencies, which is typically who we're competing against.
We're not competing against, like, Profound or AirOps, but they, you know, do things that are similar to our offering.
It's just a piece of that pie, right?
Yeah.
And how autonomous is it?
Is this something that, like, a developer, or actually not even, someone more basic and amateur than a developer, can go in and navigate this entirely?
Like, Genigo Sensor, do you need, like, a technical background to be able to navigate it?
In terms of creating the content?
Or just make sure I understand your question?
If launching this, let's say, like, on the, like, who do you work with at Augment to be able to execute this?
Oh, yeah.
We're typically working with, like, their head of content or, um...

You know, the CMO is typically like the buyer of GrowthX, and a product marketing person is really helpful because at the end of the day, we're trying to be, you know, very quickly become like experts on their business, their products and services, and their brand.
But what's great about our process is that we can ingest resources so that it's not dependent on having these meetings.
We'll just do for every single engagement a product deep dive with the product marketing team so that we can capture some of that nuance.
And if it is a very technical client, you know, sometimes we'll have, like, a 30-minute or 45-minute deeper dive with a few members of, like, their technical marketing or developer marketing team.
Okay.
And how is this – so why does a company like Augment choose you guys as opposed to, like, a typical agency or a content firm?
That, you know, what is it that – do you have a platform that you show them, or why is it that
But they are picking you guys.
Yeah, for sure.
So let me just show you like a quick example of our platform because I think that will really give a great visual.
So, you know, part of it is like the expertise that went into GrowthX.
I could give you some more details around like Marcel's story and accomplishments he's had in his career.
But the other piece of this is combining these very sophisticated workflows with experts in the loop.
And so, you know, this is our internal platform.
We've got these artifacts that we create during the first part of the engagement, that eight-week phase one that we call strategy sprint.
And we're creating these artifacts where we can get really specific about who we're creating content for, right?
So with Augment Code, the drowning engineering manager, the overwhelmed staff engineer, the frustrated senior developer.
And then there's a connection back to Augment Code, right?
right?
Augment
And we've got custom artifacts from client to client.

So with augment code, there's this proofreader checklist that gets really specific about how we write the content, which sources we should look at when we're doing research, how to come up with the right code snippets and a final quality check.
And then in these pipelines, which we stand up for each client, a lot of them are agentic.
So they'll run in a loop until we hit a certain quality threshold that we're defining with the client.
And I can show you from creating the assignments themselves to generating the brief that is going to inform how we do the research, doing the research, to creating the outline and the draft.
We're using AI to speed up that process, but we're also giving our team the ability to make changes.
So let's look at this workflow that is creating this brief for an assignment.

In the background, there's some inputs that include some direction from the managing editor on our team, those artifacts that I just went through, and a keyword or a topic that we then want to go in and write about.

And then the output is this brief, but as you see, we've built all of our workflows in code, and so if I inspect and actually dive deeper into what's happening to generate that brief, you know, we're looking at keyword traffic and organic results.
We're looking at SERP ranking factors, competitor content, search intent, and then all of that informs this brief that's generated as the output.
And so if we continue this process, and then we go in and look at, you know, one of these agentic article generation workflows, let's take the research step.
In the research step, you know, we're using specialized tools for research like Tavli or EXA.
We're leveraging, you know, different models, Anthropic, OpenAI, Perplexity.
We're not just beholden to like one model or one, you know, LLM.

We're integrating to a custom knowledge base for this client, and we store all of our knowledge base in like a knowledge graph so that we can semantically like pull the research that we've done and make this system better and better over time.
And then I'll show you a little bit behind the research, and then I'll shut up, I promise.
But you can see, like, when we do research, we store everything in this knowledge graph.
We pull it in to the research that we're doing to accelerate the process to refine how we create content.
And in this researcher step here, so what's happening, right, is we're getting all these inputs, and then we're actually going in and generating a research plan.
So it can be exploratory, hybrid, strict.
It's got an audience, an objective, questions to answer, success criteria.
Again, there's those source requirements of where we should actually do our research.
And then as it goes through the process, it's synthesizing the results, evaluating the research quality, and then again, running that research in a loop until we hit a certain quality threshold.
Storing that research in the platform and then optimizing for retrieval the next time we create the content.
As you move into actually looking at the article itself, what's great about this is that, you know, all those artifacts that we created are context for our experts in the loop.
And they can also give commands, right?
know, generate three bullet points, focus towards software developers.
And so it's not, you know, going to ChatGPT, going to a custom GPT, giving instructions and saying, right, create this type of content.
It's doing all this context engineering.
It's creating chain of thought by breaking it down into discrete.
And that's, like, a lot of why, you know, our process can really just, like, create quality consistently and, you know, generate more visibility in AI search.
Are there market hearers inside of Augment Code who use this platform, or is this something that you guys use completely to help them create the content?
Right now, just us.
I know Marcel has, like, a lot he would love to share about the platform that we're building and the way that we're bringing clients into, you know, Atlas, our content OS.
There's also, like, a profound competitor that we're launching, you know, early January, early February called Check That.
And so Marcel could tell you more about that.
But, yeah, the vision is definitely, like, we started as services, right, and we're building products as we're doing that and then moving towards more of, you know, company.
I mean, today, right, like, we're very scalable because we can use...
Use these workflows to automate a lot of the process, but we also care a lot about being able to have our experts in the loop, right, and being able to create quality at scale.
So we do as much human intervention as we can to provide more of that white glove service.
Do you normally see Aerofs and Profound as the two main competitors?
I wouldn't say I'd see them very much as competitors.

I would say it's more like other agencies, right, that I'm, like, selling against most of the time, and a lot of the times I'm just telling a story similar to the one that I'm telling you both, and obviously, you know, every client wants to know about results we've driven for our clients, and that tells a great story as well.
But I think I have to jump to another call, and I definitely want you to be able to have this conversation with Marcel.
I know he joined initially, but then dropped off, I think, like, after five minutes or so went by.
But I hope this was helpful.
I definitely wanted to at least.
You know, make your time worth the while here.
Yeah, certainly helpful.
Apologies, yeah, for us joining a bit late.
What we'll do is we'll reach back out to Marcel just to set up maybe another follow-on 30 minutes.
But this is a good overview.
I think we got a sense of what you guys are doing.
We've actually known the Augment team for a long, long time.
We're not investors in the company yet, but we certainly are big fans of them.
So it's great to hear that you guys are working with some good brands here.
Yeah, great to meet you both.
And, again, apologies that Marcel didn't join us today, but I'm glad I could at least give you some context on GrowthX.
I appreciate it.
was a fault.
Thank you, Tyler.
I appreciate it.
Have a good one.
Bye.
you both.
See you later.
Bye.
